nameMeanings.name: d'Oyley
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin): variant of Olley with fused Old French preposition d' ‘from’.

variants.name: Olley
meanings: English (East Anglia): variant of   Oley   2, a habitational name of Norman origin, from any of the five places called Ouilly in Calvados, France.

variants.name: Dilley
meanings: English: possibly a pet form of an unrecorded Old English personal name Dylla, found as the first element in the placenames Dillington (in the former Huntingdonshire) and Dilton (in Wiltshire). The name may sometimes have been confused with Doyley and Dolley (see Olley ).
German: variant of   Dilly   or a surname of French origin, an altered form of   Delay  .
In some cases also an altered form of French   Dilly  .

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert

It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

en.lifeSummary: When Richard d'Oyley was born in 1286, in Oxfordshire, England, his father, Richard d’Oyley, was 36 and his mother, Christina of Pushull, was 26. He had at least 3 sons and 1 daughter with Elizabeth Knightly. He died in 1341, in Oxfordshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 55.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard d'Oyley est né en 1286, à Oxfordshire, Angleterre, son père, Richard d’Oyley, avait 36 ans et sa mère, Christina of Pushull, avait 26 ans. Il a eu au moins 3 fils et 1 fille avec Elizabeth Knightly. Il est décédé en 1341, à Oxfordshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 55 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als Richard d'Oyley 1286 in Oxfordshire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Richard d’Oyley 36 und seine Mutter Christina of Pushull 26. Er hatte mit Elizabeth Knightly mindestens 3 Söhne und 1 Tochter. Er starb 1341 im Alter von 55 Jahren in Oxfordshire, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Richard d'Oyley è nato il nel 1286 nel Oxfordshire, England, il suo padre, Richard d’Oyley, aveva 36 anni e il suo madre, Christina of Pushull, aveva 26 anni. Lui avuto almeno 3 figli e 1 figlia con Elizabeth Knightly. Morì nel 1341, nel Oxfordshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 55 anni.

ko.lifeSummary: Richard d'Oyley(이)가 1286년 영국 스퍼드실르에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Richard d’Oyley(은)는 36세였고, 그의 어머니 Christina of Pushull(은)는 26세였다. 그는 Elizabeth Knightly(와)과 적어도 세 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1341년 영국 잉글랜드 스퍼드실르에서 55세의 나이로 사망했다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Richard d'Oyley родился в 1286 г. в местности оксфордшир, Англия, его отцу по имени Richard d’Oyley было 36 лет, а матери, которую звали Christina of Pushull, 26. У него было по крайней мере 3 сына и 1 дочь от Elizabeth Knightly. Он умер в 1341 г. в местности оксфордшир, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 55 лет.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Richard d'Oyley nació en 1286, en Oxfordshire, Inglaterra, su papá, Richard d’Oyley, tenía 36 años y su mamá, Christina of Pushull, tenía 26 años. Tuvo por lo menos 3 hijos y 1 hija con Elizabeth Knightly. Murió en 1341, en Oxfordshire, Inglaterra, Reino Unido, a la edad de 55 años.
